description: Four cognitive agents process the same task in parallel. Each uses a completely different structural algorithm — no LLM calls. The meta-controller composes verdicts using the agents' static default weights (Analytical 0.8, Creative 0.5, Adversarial 0.3, DomainExpert 0.7) with a flag-priority override layer on top. ELO learning that adapts weights over time lives in meta_controller.rs:478-520 but is out of scope for this scene's walkthrough.

agents.name: Analytical
agents.what_it_does: extracts verbs, parameters, nesting depth, contradictions. Picks ready / incomplete / contradictory / highly_nested.

agents.name: Creative
agents.what_it_does: measures bigram novelty against memory, reframing potential, cross-domain pattern applicability.

agents.name: Adversarial
agents.what_it_does: Shannon entropy + 7 CWE pattern regexes + privilege-escalation keywords + tool-specific multipliers.

agents.name: Domain Expert
agents.what_it_does: classifies the task across security / ml / infrastructure / data / api, flags boundary crossings.

agents.name: Meta-controller
agents.what_it_does: ranks by raw confidence, then applies flag overrides: adversarial block/review wins, domain boundary flags win, otherwise top-of-rank takes the route.
agents.source_lines: meta_controller.rs (ELO learning, out of scope for this scene's walkthrough)

records.summary: A destructive shell sequence with privilege escalation. Adversarial's structural risk analysis catches it without any LLM in the loop.
records.aftermath: Adversarial returns recommendation=block. The orchestrator routes this to the high-risk handler — in production the BFT consensus layer requires multi-replica agreement before any destructive tool is allowed (TDD005 BFT use case #2, Patch 90).

beats.copy: A potentially risky task. Watch how the rule-based agents split apart on this. Adversarial is the one to watch — but Analytical will weigh in too.

Analytical decomposes the task: 0 verbs found — Analytical reads English action words ("delete", "execute"), not shell binaries like rm or cat. So shell inputs come back empty here; Adversarial picks up the slack. Assessment "ready", confidence 100%.
Creative measures lateral signals: novelty 50%, reframing potential 20%, cross-domain applicability 50%. Compares against 0 remembered tasks. Confidence 41%.

Adversarial runs the risk path: Shannon entropy 4.21, tool multiplier 1.0, 2 flags (cwe78 os command injection + privilege escalation:sudo). CWE is the standard catalog of common software weaknesses — the regex caught a known injection pattern. Recommendation "block". Risk score 65%.
DomainExpert classifies across the taxonomy: primary configured "security", best-detected "general". 0 domains touched; no boundary flags. Confidence 30%.
Meta-controller composes. Raw confidence rank: analytical 100% > creative 41% > adversarial 35% > domain_expert 30%. But raw confidence isn't the only signal — flags can override the ranking.

Selected: adversarial via flag override. adversarial recommended "block" — security priority overrides confidence ranking. Total cost: ~8.5ms across four parallel structural agents (no LLM calls). A 4-LLM ensemble running in parallel would have paid roughly 50ms wall-clock for the same diversity.

meta.selection_reason: adversarial recommended "block" — security priority overrides confidence ranking

records.task_description: Deploy the kubernetes cluster with terraform and configure tls certificates for the admin endpoint
records.summary: An infrastructure task that crosses domains — Kubernetes + Terraform (infrastructure) + TLS + admin endpoint (security). DomainExpert flags the security-infrastructure crossover that single-domain reviewers miss.
records.aftermath: DomainExpert raises the security_infra_crossover flag. The orchestrator routes through both the security review and infra review queues — neither alone would catch the boundary risk.

beats.copy: A potentially risky task. Watch how the rule-based agents split apart on this. Adversarial is the one to watch — but Analytical will weigh in too.
Analytical decomposes the task: 2 verbs, 0 parameters, nesting depth 0, ambiguity 0.00. Assessment "ready". Confidence 100%.
Creative measures lateral signals: novelty 50%, reframing potential 20%, cross-domain applicability 50%. Compares against 0 remembered tasks. Confidence 41%.
Adversarial runs the risk path: Shannon entropy 4.17, tool multiplier 1.0, 1 flag (privilege escalation:admin). Recommendation "allow". Risk score 20%.
DomainExpert classifies across the taxonomy: primary configured "security", best-detected "infrastructure". 3 domains touched; cross domain:infrastructure+security. Confidence 41%.
Meta-controller composes. Raw confidence rank: analytical 100% > adversarial 80% > creative 41% > domain_expert 41%. But raw confidence isn't the only signal — flags can override the ranking.

Selected: domain_expert via flag override. domain_expert flagged cross_domain:infrastructure+security, security_infra_crossover — boundary risk routed. Total cost: ~8.5ms across four parallel structural agents (no LLM calls). A 4-LLM ensemble running in parallel would have paid roughly 50ms wall-clock for the same diversity.

meta.selection_reason: domain_expert flagged cross_domain:infrastructure+security, security_infra_crossover — boundary risk routed

records.task_description: Find a way to optimize the encoding pipeline efficiently and improve the embedding model performance
records.summary: An ML optimization task that touches infrastructure (pipeline) and ML (embed, model). Creative spots the reframing potential — but DomainExpert's cross-domain flag has routing priority. The flag, not raw confidence, decides where this goes.
records.aftermath: DomainExpert overrides via the cross-domain:ml+infrastructure flag. Creative's signals (52% reframing potential, 68% cross-domain applicability) are still recorded in the audit trail — the orchestrator can use them later if the routed pipeline asks for alternatives.

beats.copy: An ML-flavored task. The taxonomy agent and the lateral agent both have signals here. DomainExpert's taxonomy is built for this; Creative looks for reframings nearby.
Analytical decomposes the task: 1 verb, 0 parameters, nesting depth 0, ambiguity 0.00. Assessment "ready". Confidence 100%.
Creative measures lateral signals: novelty 50%, reframing potential 52%, cross-domain applicability 68%. Compares against 0 remembered tasks. Confidence 56%.
Adversarial runs the risk path: Shannon entropy 4.08, tool multiplier 1.0, 0 flags (no flags). Recommendation "allow". Risk score 0%.
DomainExpert classifies across the taxonomy: primary configured "ml", best-detected "ml". 2 domains touched; cross domain:ml+infrastructure. Confidence 48%.
Meta-controller composes. Raw confidence rank: analytical 100% > adversarial 100% > creative 56% > domain_expert 48%. But raw confidence isn't the only signal — flags can override the ranking.

Selected: domain_expert via flag override. domain_expert flagged cross_domain:ml+infrastructure — boundary risk routed. Total cost: ~8.5ms across four parallel structural agents (no LLM calls). A 4-LLM ensemble running in parallel would have paid roughly 50ms wall-clock for the same diversity.

meta.selection_reason: domain_expert flagged cross_domain:ml+infrastructure — boundary risk routed

records.task_description: Create file /tmp/output.txt with the analysis report and validate the schema before writing
records.summary: A well-formed task with clear verbs (create, validate, write), explicit parameters (/tmp/output.txt), and no contradictions. Analytical wins because the structural completeness check is what this task needs.
records.aftermath: Analytical returns assessment=ready and high confidence. The task proceeds with no further routing — clear inputs don't need cognitive escalation.

beats.copy: A clear, well-formed task. The kind where the cheap agents do all the work. Analytical does the heavy lifting on structurally clean inputs like this one.
Analytical decomposes the task: 2 verbs, 1 parameter, nesting depth 0, ambiguity 0.00. Assessment "ready". Confidence 100%.
Creative measures lateral signals: novelty 50%, reframing potential 20%, cross-domain applicability 68%. Compares against 0 remembered tasks. Confidence 46%.
Adversarial runs the risk path: Shannon entropy 4.27, tool multiplier 1.0, 0 flags (no flags). Recommendation "allow". Risk score 0%.
DomainExpert classifies across the taxonomy: primary configured "data", best-detected "data". 1 domain touched; no boundary flags. Confidence 44%.
Meta-controller composes. Raw confidence rank: analytical 100% > adversarial 100% > creative 46% > domain_expert 44%. No flags forced an override — top-of-rank takes the route.

Selected: analytical at 100%. The task was well-formed — verbs match parameters, no contradictions, low ambiguity. No flag forced an override. Total cost: ~8.5ms; a 4-LLM ensemble running in parallel would have been ~50ms wall-clock.

meta.selection_reason: highest raw confidence (100%); no flags forced an override

records.task_description: do the thing with the stuff
records.summary: The honest failure case. Structural analysis can't detect vagueness — none of the four agents have a 'this task means nothing' signal. Analytical returns high confidence because there are no NEEDS_OBJECT verbs to be incomplete about. This is what the limitation looks like.
records.aftermath: All four agents converge on high confidence with zero flags. The structural-only path the demo runs selects analytical and proceeds — visible in the walkthrough's verdict. The real safety net for this case lives one layer up: in production, the meta-controller's conformity detector (meta_controller.rs:412-476) compares LLM-generated response bigrams across agents. When responses cluster too tightly without any agent flagging anything, the detector fires and triggers a curiosity_probe asking the user to clarify. That detector needs LLM output to run; this scene shows only the structural verdicts that feed into it. Honest scope: the limitation is real, the detector that catches it is not part of this walkthrough.

beats.copy: A vague task. Each agent will surface something different — or nothing at all. If the input is too thin, none of the four can read intent. Expect convergence, not insight.
Analytical decomposes the task: 0 verbs, 0 parameters, nesting depth 0, ambiguity 0.00. Assessment "ready". Confidence 100%.
Creative measures lateral signals: novelty 50%, reframing potential 20%, cross-domain applicability 50%. Compares against 0 remembered tasks. Confidence 41%.
Adversarial runs the risk path: Shannon entropy 3.54, tool multiplier 1.0, 0 flags (no flags). Recommendation "allow". Risk score 0%.
DomainExpert classifies across the taxonomy: primary configured "general", best-detected "general". 0 domains touched; no boundary flags. Confidence 40%.

All four agents converged on high confidence with zero flags. That's a tell — when rule-based agents agree without surfacing anything, the input was too thin for them to read. The orchestrator has to escalate beyond the structural layer.

Selected: analytical (the system's tie-break default). The honest read: every agent gave a confident verdict on a task that means nothing, because rule-based analysis can't detect vagueness. In production this is where the orchestrator would escalate to a clarification request — that path is shown in the aftermath, not this walkthrough.

meta.selection_reason: highest raw confidence (100%); no flags forced an override
